I recall something about prefix sums and modular arithmetic. Oh right! The idea is that if two prefix sums have the same remainder when divided by k, then the subarray between them has a sum that's a multiple of k. 3. For each current remainder, if it's already in the map, check if the previous index is at least one less than the current index. If so, return true.
4. Also, if the current remainder is zero and the current index is at least 1 (since the subarray needs to be at least two elements), return true.

Now, considering the constraints, the time complexity is O(n), since we're doing a single pass through the array, and each operation (hash map access) is O(1) average case.

Space complexity is O(k), since the hash map can store up to k different remainders. But since k can be up to 2^31-1, in the worst case, the space is O(n), but in practice, it's O(k), which is acceptable.

solution: 
```class Solution {
public:
    bool checkSubarraySum(vector<int>& nums, int k) {
        unordered_map<int, int> prefix;
        int current_sum = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < nums.size(); ++i) {
            current_sum += nums[i];
            int mod = current_sum % k;
            mod = (mod + k) % k; // Ensure non-negative
            if (mod == 0 && i >= 1) {
                return true;
            }
            if (prefix.find(mod) != prefix.end()) {
                if (i - prefix[mod] >= 2) {
                    return true;
                }
            } else {
                prefix[mod] = i;
            }
        }
        return false;
    }
};
```

efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(min(n, k))
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses prefix sums and a hash map to track remainders, ensuring each element is processed once. It efficiently checks for subarrays with sums divisible by k, avoiding unnecessary calculations and ensuring optimal performance.
